blocks:
Ayahs

قُلْ أَعُوذُ بِرَبِّ ٱلنَّاسِ (۱)
Say, ‘I seek the protection of the Lord of humans, (1)
مَلِكِ ٱلنَّاسِ (۲)
Sovereign of humans, (2)
إِلَـٰهِ ٱلنَّاسِ (۳)
God of humans, (3)
مِن شَرِّ ٱلْوَسْوَاسِ ٱلْخَنَّاسِ (٤)
from the evil of the sneaky tempter (4)
ٱلَّذِى يُوَسْوِسُ فِى صُدُورِ ٱلنَّاسِ (٥)
who puts temptations into the breasts of humans, (5)
مِنَ ٱلْجِنَّةِ وَٱلنَّاسِ (٦)
from among the jinn and humans.’ (6)

Key Points

In the Quran, the word "Qul" (Say) has appeared more than 300 times, many of which are commands from Allah to the Prophet in response to opponents or supporters.

The word waswas refers both to the entity that whispers (the tempter) and to the whispering itself (evil thoughts and notions), but here it refers to the tempter.

Saying the word A'udhu (I seek refuge) is not enough to be saved from dangers; rather, one must also practically avoid the factors that cause danger. Otherwise, building a house in the path of a flood and writing A'udhu billah min al-sayl (I seek refuge in Allah from the flood) over the door is tantamount to mockery. Whoever says, "A'udhu billah," must also practically distance himself from the sources of corruption.

The first thing perceptible to man is his growth, perfection, and nurturing, (Rabb an-Nas - Lord of mankind); next is governance, administration, and rule, (Malik an-Nas - Sovereign of mankind); and once his growth is elevated, it is worship and devotion, (Ilah an-Nas - God of mankind).

One must seek refuge in Him Who knows the secrets and inner whisperings. "He knows the deceit of the eyes and what the breasts conceal." (40:19) He is aware of the treachery of the eyes and what the hearts conceal. (282)

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: Satan rests upon the heart of man; whenever he remembers Allah, he hides, and whenever he becomes heedless, he whispers to him. "Fa idha dhakara al-'abdu Allah khannasa... wa idha ghafala waswasa" (283)

Those who cast doubt and temptation upon others are performing a satanic act. The opponents of Prophet Salih used to ask the people: Do you know that Salih is a messenger? And with this statement, they created doubt among the people. "A-ta'lamūna anna Ṣāliḥan mursal" (7:75) (284)

At the beginning of the Quran, we sought help from Allah with Bismillah (In the Name of Allah), and at the end of the Quran, we seek refuge in Allah.

Khannas derives from khunūs, meaning to hide or retreat. Satan is both hidden himself and his work is hidden; if his whispering were public, he would not dominate people, but through pretense and justification, he appears in a beautiful guise and succeeds. Imam Ali عليه السلام says: Satan mixes truth and falsehood by making them appear similar, and in this way, he prevails over his followers. "Fa hunālika yastawlī ash-Shayṭānu ‘alā awliyā’ih" (285)

Since Satan is the Khannas (the one who retreats and returns), he keeps coming and going until he succeeds; therefore, we must also increase our remembrance of Allah.

The Quran repeatedly criticizes man that whenever he is afflicted, he prays and seeks refuge, but as soon as the danger is removed, it is as if He does not know Us. (286)

In this Surah, because the whispering takes root inside the human soul and heart and plays with our faith, intellect, and thought, the Name of Allah is mentioned three times: Rabb an-Nas, Malik an-Nas, Ilah an-Nas. However, in Surah Al-Falaq, where the evils are external to the breasts, the Name of Allah is mentioned only once: Rabb al-Falaq (Lord of the Daybreak). Indeed, the danger of intellectual deviation and its effect on the soul is far greater than external dangers, and cultural and intellectual enemies are more insidious and dangerous than military and economic enemies.

It is narrated in traditions that Satan became distressed by Allah's acceptance of man's repentance. He gathered his helpers and sought their assistance. Each one offered an idea, but one of them said: I will whisper to man and make him forget repentance. Iblis approved of this plan of forgetting repentance. (287)

Now that He is Rabb an-Nas (Lord of mankind), we should not accept the methods of nurturing from others. Now that He is Malik an-Nas (Sovereign of mankind), we should not enslave ourselves to others. And now that He is Ilah an-Nas (God of mankind), we should not attach our hearts to anything other than Him. This thought and belief is the best means of seeking refuge from whisperings.

The one who whispers in the breasts, hearts, and souls of people may be from the race of Jinn and Satan, or from the race of man. Indeed, temptations, promises, and procrastination are among the ways of whispering.

Satan's whispering to Prophet Adam and other friends of Allah is limited to the casting or projection of the temptation: "Fa waswasa ilayhi ash-Shayṭān" (20:120), (288) "Alqā ash-Shayṭānu fī umniyyatihi" (22:52). (289) But regarding the general public, it is the entry and penetration of the whispering into their hearts and souls: "Yuwaswisu fī ṣudūr an-nās." However, this is not in the form of absolute domination over the human heart such that there is no escape from it. For the Quran states in another verse: "Inna alladhīna ittaqaw idhā massahum ṭā’ifun mina ash-Shayṭāni tadhakkarū fa idhā hum mubṣirūn" (7:201). (290) When the devils approach the pious individuals to tempt them through contact, they become aware and do not allow penetration.

The repetition of the word Nas (mankind) in Rabb an-Nas – Malik an-Nas – Ilah an-Nas indicates that the Lordship, Sovereignty, and Divinity of Allah are universal and are not exclusive to a specific individual, group, or race of mankind.

First came Rabb an-Nas, then Malik an-Nas, and then Ilah an-Nas. Perhaps this is because what is closer and more tangible to human nature is seeking refuge in the Nurturer: Rabb an-Nas.

Just as children, when in danger, first call out to their mother, then to the one who has power (Malik an-Nas), and finally, to the source of mercy and life (Ilah an-Nas).

Allah is aware of man's needs and the dangers that threaten him, but the Divine method of nurturing is that man should express his need, his plea for help, and his seeking of refuge verbally, and instill in himself the realization of his poverty and need, so that the spirit of humility, devotion, and submission may blossom within him. "Qul A'udhu..."

According to the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, the thoughts and whisperings that involuntarily occur to a person are not held against him, as long as the person does not act upon them or speak them. "Wuḍi‘a ‘an ummatī mā ḥaddathat bihi nafsahā mā lam ya‘mal bihi aw yatakallam" (291).

282) Ghafir, 19.

283) Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 67, p. 49.

284) Al-A'raf, 75.

285) Nahj al-Balaghah, Sermon 50.

286) Yunus, 11.

287) Tafsir Nur ath-Thaqalayn.

288) Taha, 120.

289) Al-Hajj, 52.

290) Al-A'raf, 201.

291) Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 17, p. 54.

292) Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 89, p. 209.

Messages

  1. The Prophet is the trustee of revelation and speaks nothing of his own accord. (Qul)

  2. The dangers are so severe that Allah commands His Prophet to seek refuge. (Qul A'udhu)

  3. Without seeking help from Allah, it is impossible to combat evils. (A'udhu bi-Rabb an-Nas)

  4. Sinners should not despair, because Allah is the Lord of all mankind, not just the believers. (Rabb an-Nas)

  5. When the Prophet seeks refuge in Allah, our duty is clear. (Qul A'udhu bi-Rabb an-Nas)

  6. Man must consider himself under the nurturing of Allah (Rabb an-Nas), accept His sovereignty and rule (Malik an-Nas), and take Him as his object of worship (Ilah an-Nas).

  7. The faithless man seeks refuge in his own power, population, ethnicity, and wealth, but the men of Allah seek refuge in the Lord, the Sovereign, and the God of existence. (Rabb an-Nas – Malik an-Nas – Ilah an-Nas)

  8. The greatest dangers are the most hidden ones, which are the inner whisperings. (Min sharr al-waswās al-khannās) "And praise be to Allah, the Lord of the Worlds."
